title: practical english usage - Part 301
content:
The temperature is three degrees below zero.

where we  think of a vertical scale.

Below is used in measurements of temperature and height, and in other cases 4 measurements:          below not The whole village is under water, (     ... below water.) What are you wearing under your sweater?

I think the cat's under the bed.

when things are touching.

We   prefer under when something is covered or hidden by what is over it, and 3 covered:     under A moment later the sun had disappeared below the horizon.

The climbers stopped 300m  below the top of the mountain.

We   prefer below when one thing is not directly under another.

2 not   directly      under:    below Look in the cupboard below/under the sink.

The  prepositions below and under can both mean ‘ lower than'.

lower    than':  below         under 399 below,       under,       underneath            and   beneath For infinitives and -ing forms after begin and start, ►     105,10.

The President's wife fired the gun to start the race.

How do you start the washing machine?

c   'make something start' The car won't start.

b  'start working’  (for machines) I think we ought to start at six, while the roads are empty.

a  'start a journey1 Start (but not begin) is used to mean: 2 cases    where     begin      is  not   possible Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  400  beside and besides I'll bet you she's not at home.

I'll bet... is also possible.

(More natural than I bet (you) that she's not at home.) I bet (you) she's not at home.

That is usually dropped.

I bet (you) can be used in an informal style to mean ‘ I think it's probable that’ .

It's too late to go out now. Besides, it's starting to rain.

/ don't like those shoes; besides, they're too expensive.

at the beginning of a clause.

conclusive argument to what has gone before. In this case, besides usually goes ‘ also’ , ‘ as well’ , ‘ in any case'. It is often used in speech to add a stronger, more Besides can also be used informally as a discourse marker (►   301.2) meaning Who was at the party besides Jack and the Bensons?

Besides literature, we have to study history and philosophy.

what is already known.

Besides can be used like as well as (►  382), when we  add new information to They lived in a little house beside the river.

Beside is a preposition meaning ‘ at the side of, ‘ by’ , ‘ next to’ .

400 beside       and   besides ►    354 for details.

The  difference between above and over is similar to the difference between below and under.

in the text’ .

In a book or a paper, see below means ‘ look at something written later I can't take my sweater off -I haven't got anything on underneath.

In other cases we  prefer underneath for adverbial use.

A lot of businesses are going under because of the economic crisis.

Under can be used as an adverb particle (►   195) with some verbs.

We looked over the cliff at the waves crashing on the rocks below.

Below can be used as an adverb.

Her behaviour is beneath contempt. (= really disgraceful) He acts as if I was beneath his notice. (= not worth considering) It is common before abstract nouns in some fixed expressions.

The ship sank slowly beneath the waves.

Beneath is used mostly in a rather literary style.

vocabulary •  403   between and among Your passport is somewhere among all these papers.           -> - I saw something between the wheels of the car.

His house is hidden among the trees.

- Our  house is between the woods, the river and the village.

a crowd or a mass of people or things which  we  do not see separately. Compare: people or things. We   prefer among when somebody or something is in a group, We   usually say that somebody or something is between several clearly separate 2  between          or  among        more    than    two There seems to be less and less time between each birthday.

Between is common before each.

I'll be at the office between nine and eleven.

We need two metres between the windows.

Between is often used to talk about distances or intervals.

a long valley between high mountains She was standing between Alice and Emily.
